catalyst.source: 0.5 g of catalyst was added to the photo-reactor, and 30 ml of anhydrous acetonitrile (purified by calcium hydroxide and distilled before using) and 1 mmol of aromatic alcohol (purified by distillation before using) was subsequently put into the reactor.
catalyst.context: 2.1 The preparation of titanium dioxide nanoparticles TiO 2 nanoparticles were prepared using a modified sol-gel method.In a typical synthesis procedure, a volume of 20 ml of tetrabutyl titanate was dissolved in 80 ml of anhydrous diethyl ether at room temperature, and then 24 ml of acetic acid was added.After stirring this solution for 30 min, 30 ml of a mixture solution of double deionized water and acetic acid with a ratio of 2 : 1 (by volume) was added dropwise to the precursor solution under vigorous stirring.Following this, a large amount of white precipitate was formed in the solution.Subsequently the mixture was kept stirring for 2 hours.The suspension was aged under airtight conditions at room temperature for 24 hours.After suction filtration, the xerogel was washed several times with deionized water to remove the residual acid, and then dried at 100 C for one day.Finally, the samples were divided into several sections and calcined at 400-800 C for 2 hours in a muffle furnace at a 5 C per minute heating rate from room to setting temperature.Then, the sample was ground into a power in an agate mortar. AgTiO 2 nanoparticles were produced by a photodeposition method.By using the above synthetic TiO 2 nanoparticles as the catalyst support, 1 g of TiO 2 was dispersed in an oxalic acid aqueous solution and sonicated for 15 min, then a predetermined amount of AgNO 3 was added to the solution and stirred for 2 hours to form a homogeneous solution.Then this solution was irradiated on an ultraviolet lamp (PHILIPS TUV 25 W G2578 bactericidal lamp) for 4 hours under stirring at 500 rpm at room temperature.When its colour changed from white to reddish brown, the suspension was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm and washed several times with deionized water.The xerogel was transferred to an electronic vacuum oven and dried overnight at 60 C. XRD measurements were performed on a D8 diffractometer from Bruker instruments (Cu K radiation, 0.15406 nm) operated at 40 kV and 25 mA.The diffractograms were collected in the 2 range from 20 to 80in steps of 0.02per min with a count time of 20 s at each point.The crystalline phase can be determined from the integration intensities of the anatase and rutile phases corresponding to (101), ( 110) and (120), respectively.The average crystalline sizes were determined according to the Debye-Scherrer equation using the full width half maximum data of each phase.The nanoparticle average crystalline size of TiO 2 was determined according to the 2 25.3(101)face.The SEM morphological analysis of the samples was carried out using a scanning electron microscope (Quanta 200 F FEI). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEM 2100 F transmission electron microscope equipped with EDX at an operating voltage of 200 kV.The TEM samples were prepared by sonication of the powders in absolute alcohol for 15 minutes and subsequently dispersed on carbon coated copper grids dried at room temperature.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-vis DSR) were recorded using a SHIMADU UV-2550 UV-vis spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere attachment for the diffuse reflectance in the range of 300-800 nm and using BaSO 4 powder as the reference.The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded using a Perkin-Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature.The nanocomposites were dispersed in carbon tetrachloride and excited using D light at a wavelength of 265 nm. The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction coupled with aromatic alcohol oxidation reactions were carried out in a 70 ml homemade double-jacket quartz glass reactor.0.5 g of catalyst was added to the photo-reactor, and 30 ml of anhydrous acetonitrile (purified by calcium hydroxide and distilled before using) and 1 mmol of aromatic alcohol (purified by distillation before using) was subsequently put into the reactor.After that, the reaction cell was evacuated to a base pressure of 10 kPa for 15 minutes to remove residual oxygen, and afterwards high-purity CO 2 was filled into the reactor for 30 minutes at a flow rate of 15 ml per minute.To ensure the full removal of trace amounts of oxygen, this operation was repeated three times.In order to remove trace amounts of H 2 O and oxygen in the gas, the CO 2 gas was purified through allochroic silica gel, asbestos and activated carbon before importing into the reactor.Then, a balloon was connected to the reactor keeping the reaction under 0.1 MPa pressure.Before irradiation the suspension was kept stirring at 500 rpm for 60 minutes to ensure a CO 2 adsorption and desorption equilibrium.The ultraviolet (UV) light (high pressure mercury lamp main wavelength is 365 nm) was irradiated from the box wall 10 cm in front of the reactor.The liquid products were analyzed using gas chromatography (GC) with a flame ionization detector (FID).In order to analyse the gas products qualitatively, the gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS) (7890A-5975C Agilent Corporation equipped with a HP-5 capillary column (Hewllett-Packard, USA, 30 m 0.25 mm 0.25 mm)) was also used to identify the components of the product.The liquid products were also characterized using a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer (NMR).

catalyst.source: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with selective alcohol oxidation was catalyzed by AgTiO 2 nanocomposites under UV light irradiation at room temperature and ambient pressure.
catalyst.context: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with selective alcohol oxidation was catalyzed by AgTiO 2 nanocomposites under UV light irradiation at room temperature and ambient pressure.CO 2 as the oxidant was reduced to methanol by photo-induced electrons.Aromatic alcohols were used as the reductant to react with photo-generated holes, and converted to aromatic aldehydes with high selectivity.The optimal catalyst was ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 nanoparticles.The maximum conversion of aromatic alcohol to aldehyde was 91.7 with a selectivity of 98, and the highest yield of methanol was 358.7 mol g -1 after irradiation for 48 hours.Our findings may help to design more active photocatalytic systems for the reduction of carbon dioxide and to introduce an environmentally friendly strategy for selective alcohol oxidation based on photocatalysis. Fig. 1 (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 nanoparticles.(c and d) HR-TEM images of ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 nanoparticles. Fig.2The 1 H NMR spectra of (a) the standard CH 3 OH and (b) the product (MeOH) as catalyzed by ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 under 0.1 MPa of CO 2 after irradiation for 48 hours. Fig. 3 (a) The effect of irradiation time on the conversion of benzyl alcohol and the selectivity for benzaldehyde.(b) The yield of methanol production (unit: mol g -1 ) as a function of irradiation time.(Reaction conditions: 30 ml of CH 3 CN, 1 mmol of benzyl alcohol, 50 mg of catalyst). Fig.4Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methanol coupled with alcohol oxidation with the 3 wt AgTiO 2 catalyst after three run cycles. Production yields of the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with selective benzyl alcohol oxidation under UV light irradiation using TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 catalysts a 2 to methanol coupled with alcohol oxidation is as follows: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methanol coupled with alcohol oxidation with the 3 wt AgTiO 2 catalyst a TiO 2 , stirred in 40 ml of CH 3 CN, under 0.1 MPa CO 2 and ultraviolet light (main wavelength of 365 nm) irradiation. a Reaction conditions: alcohol (1 mmol), 3 wt Agb RT: reaction time.cUnit:mol g-1.d Conversion, of alcohol.e Selectivity of benzaldehyde.f The ratio of the formation of methanol and aldehyde. This journal is The Royal Society of Chemistry 2015 a Reaction conditions: 40 ml of CH 3 CN, 1 mmol of benzyl alcohol.b Reaction time.c Reaction conditions: 40 ml of benzyl alcohol.d Reaction conditions: 40 ml of CH 3 CN, without benzyl alcohol. The support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21363014, 21106059 and 21103082) is gratefully acknowledged.This work was also supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (grant no.20142BCB23003 and 20143ACB21021).

lamp.llm generation: PHILIPS TUV 25 W G2578 bactericidal lamp
lamp.source: Then this solution was irradiated on an ultraviolet lamp (PHILIPS TUV 25 W G2578 bactericidal lamp) for 4 hours under stirring at 500 rpm at room temperature.
lamp.context: 2.1 The preparation of titanium dioxide nanoparticles TiO 2 nanoparticles were prepared using a modified sol-gel method.In a typical synthesis procedure, a volume of 20 ml of tetrabutyl titanate was dissolved in 80 ml of anhydrous diethyl ether at room temperature, and then 24 ml of acetic acid was added.After stirring this solution for 30 min, 30 ml of a mixture solution of double deionized water and acetic acid with a ratio of 2 : 1 (by volume) was added dropwise to the precursor solution under vigorous stirring.Following this, a large amount of white precipitate was formed in the solution.Subsequently the mixture was kept stirring for 2 hours.The suspension was aged under airtight conditions at room temperature for 24 hours.After suction filtration, the xerogel was washed several times with deionized water to remove the residual acid, and then dried at 100 C for one day.Finally, the samples were divided into several sections and calcined at 400-800 C for 2 hours in a muffle furnace at a 5 C per minute heating rate from room to setting temperature.Then, the sample was ground into a power in an agate mortar. AgTiO 2 nanoparticles were produced by a photodeposition method.By using the above synthetic TiO 2 nanoparticles as the catalyst support, 1 g of TiO 2 was dispersed in an oxalic acid aqueous solution and sonicated for 15 min, then a predetermined amount of AgNO 3 was added to the solution and stirred for 2 hours to form a homogeneous solution.Then this solution was irradiated on an ultraviolet lamp (PHILIPS TUV 25 W G2578 bactericidal lamp) for 4 hours under stirring at 500 rpm at room temperature.When its colour changed from white to reddish brown, the suspension was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm and washed several times with deionized water.The xerogel was transferred to an electronic vacuum oven and dried overnight at 60 C. XRD measurements were performed on a D8 diffractometer from Bruker instruments (Cu K radiation, 0.15406 nm) operated at 40 kV and 25 mA.The diffractograms were collected in the 2 range from 20 to 80in steps of 0.02per min with a count time of 20 s at each point.The crystalline phase can be determined from the integration intensities of the anatase and rutile phases corresponding to (101), ( 110) and (120), respectively.The average crystalline sizes were determined according to the Debye-Scherrer equation using the full width half maximum data of each phase.The nanoparticle average crystalline size of TiO 2 was determined according to the 2 25.3(101)face.The SEM morphological analysis of the samples was carried out using a scanning electron microscope (Quanta 200 F FEI). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEM 2100 F transmission electron microscope equipped with EDX at an operating voltage of 200 kV.The TEM samples were prepared by sonication of the powders in absolute alcohol for 15 minutes and subsequently dispersed on carbon coated copper grids dried at room temperature.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-vis DSR) were recorded using a SHIMADU UV-2550 UV-vis spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere attachment for the diffuse reflectance in the range of 300-800 nm and using BaSO 4 powder as the reference.The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded using a Perkin-Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature.The nanocomposites were dispersed in carbon tetrachloride and excited using D light at a wavelength of 265 nm. The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction coupled with aromatic alcohol oxidation reactions were carried out in a 70 ml homemade double-jacket quartz glass reactor.0.5 g of catalyst was added to the photo-reactor, and 30 ml of anhydrous acetonitrile (purified by calcium hydroxide and distilled before using) and 1 mmol of aromatic alcohol (purified by distillation before using) was subsequently put into the reactor.After that, the reaction cell was evacuated to a base pressure of 10 kPa for 15 minutes to remove residual oxygen, and afterwards high-purity CO 2 was filled into the reactor for 30 minutes at a flow rate of 15 ml per minute.To ensure the full removal of trace amounts of oxygen, this operation was repeated three times.In order to remove trace amounts of H 2 O and oxygen in the gas, the CO 2 gas was purified through allochroic silica gel, asbestos and activated carbon before importing into the reactor.Then, a balloon was connected to the reactor keeping the reaction under 0.1 MPa pressure.Before irradiation the suspension was kept stirring at 500 rpm for 60 minutes to ensure a CO 2 adsorption and desorption equilibrium.The ultraviolet (UV) light (high pressure mercury lamp main wavelength is 365 nm) was irradiated from the box wall 10 cm in front of the reactor.The liquid products were analyzed using gas chromatography (GC) with a flame ionization detector (FID).In order to analyse the gas products qualitatively, the gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS) (7890A-5975C Agilent Corporation equipped with a HP-5 capillary column (Hewllett-Packard, USA, 30 m 0.25 mm 0.25 mm)) was also used to identify the components of the product.The liquid products were also characterized using a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer (NMR).

reaction medium.context: 2.1 The preparation of titanium dioxide nanoparticles TiO 2 nanoparticles were prepared using a modified sol-gel method.In a typical synthesis procedure, a volume of 20 ml of tetrabutyl titanate was dissolved in 80 ml of anhydrous diethyl ether at room temperature, and then 24 ml of acetic acid was added.After stirring this solution for 30 min, 30 ml of a mixture solution of double deionized water and acetic acid with a ratio of 2 : 1 (by volume) was added dropwise to the precursor solution under vigorous stirring.Following this, a large amount of white precipitate was formed in the solution.Subsequently the mixture was kept stirring for 2 hours.The suspension was aged under airtight conditions at room temperature for 24 hours.After suction filtration, the xerogel was washed several times with deionized water to remove the residual acid, and then dried at 100 C for one day.Finally, the samples were divided into several sections and calcined at 400-800 C for 2 hours in a muffle furnace at a 5 C per minute heating rate from room to setting temperature.Then, the sample was ground into a power in an agate mortar. AgTiO 2 nanoparticles were produced by a photodeposition method.By using the above synthetic TiO 2 nanoparticles as the catalyst support, 1 g of TiO 2 was dispersed in an oxalic acid aqueous solution and sonicated for 15 min, then a predetermined amount of AgNO 3 was added to the solution and stirred for 2 hours to form a homogeneous solution.Then this solution was irradiated on an ultraviolet lamp (PHILIPS TUV 25 W G2578 bactericidal lamp) for 4 hours under stirring at 500 rpm at room temperature.When its colour changed from white to reddish brown, the suspension was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm and washed several times with deionized water.The xerogel was transferred to an electronic vacuum oven and dried overnight at 60 C. XRD measurements were performed on a D8 diffractometer from Bruker instruments (Cu K radiation, 0.15406 nm) operated at 40 kV and 25 mA.The diffractograms were collected in the 2 range from 20 to 80in steps of 0.02per min with a count time of 20 s at each point.The crystalline phase can be determined from the integration intensities of the anatase and rutile phases corresponding to (101), ( 110) and (120), respectively.The average crystalline sizes were determined according to the Debye-Scherrer equation using the full width half maximum data of each phase.The nanoparticle average crystalline size of TiO 2 was determined according to the 2 25.3(101)face.The SEM morphological analysis of the samples was carried out using a scanning electron microscope (Quanta 200 F FEI). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEM 2100 F transmission electron microscope equipped with EDX at an operating voltage of 200 kV.The TEM samples were prepared by sonication of the powders in absolute alcohol for 15 minutes and subsequently dispersed on carbon coated copper grids dried at room temperature.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-vis DSR) were recorded using a SHIMADU UV-2550 UV-vis spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere attachment for the diffuse reflectance in the range of 300-800 nm and using BaSO 4 powder as the reference.The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded using a Perkin-Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer at room temperature.The nanocomposites were dispersed in carbon tetrachloride and excited using D light at a wavelength of 265 nm. The photocatalytic CO 2 reduction coupled with aromatic alcohol oxidation reactions were carried out in a 70 ml homemade double-jacket quartz glass reactor.0.5 g of catalyst was added to the photo-reactor, and 30 ml of anhydrous acetonitrile (purified by calcium hydroxide and distilled before using) and 1 mmol of aromatic alcohol (purified by distillation before using) was subsequently put into the reactor.After that, the reaction cell was evacuated to a base pressure of 10 kPa for 15 minutes to remove residual oxygen, and afterwards high-purity CO 2 was filled into the reactor for 30 minutes at a flow rate of 15 ml per minute.To ensure the full removal of trace amounts of oxygen, this operation was repeated three times.In order to remove trace amounts of H 2 O and oxygen in the gas, the CO 2 gas was purified through allochroic silica gel, asbestos and activated carbon before importing into the reactor.Then, a balloon was connected to the reactor keeping the reaction under 0.1 MPa pressure.Before irradiation the suspension was kept stirring at 500 rpm for 60 minutes to ensure a CO 2 adsorption and desorption equilibrium.The ultraviolet (UV) light (high pressure mercury lamp main wavelength is 365 nm) was irradiated from the box wall 10 cm in front of the reactor.The liquid products were analyzed using gas chromatography (GC) with a flame ionization detector (FID).In order to analyse the gas products qualitatively, the gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC-MS) (7890A-5975C Agilent Corporation equipped with a HP-5 capillary column (Hewllett-Packard, USA, 30 m 0.25 mm 0.25 mm)) was also used to identify the components of the product.The liquid products were also characterized using a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer (NMR).

reaction medium.source: (Reaction conditions: 30 ml of CH 3 CN, 1 mmol of benzyl alcohol, 50 mg of catalyst).
reaction medium.context: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with selective alcohol oxidation was catalyzed by AgTiO 2 nanocomposites under UV light irradiation at room temperature and ambient pressure.CO 2 as the oxidant was reduced to methanol by photo-induced electrons.Aromatic alcohols were used as the reductant to react with photo-generated holes, and converted to aromatic aldehydes with high selectivity.The optimal catalyst was ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 nanoparticles.The maximum conversion of aromatic alcohol to aldehyde was 91.7 with a selectivity of 98, and the highest yield of methanol was 358.7 mol g -1 after irradiation for 48 hours.Our findings may help to design more active photocatalytic systems for the reduction of carbon dioxide and to introduce an environmentally friendly strategy for selective alcohol oxidation based on photocatalysis. Fig. 1 (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 nanoparticles.(c and d) HR-TEM images of ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 nanoparticles. Fig.2The 1 H NMR spectra of (a) the standard CH 3 OH and (b) the product (MeOH) as catalyzed by ca. 3 wt AgTiO 2 under 0.1 MPa of CO 2 after irradiation for 48 hours. Fig. 3 (a) The effect of irradiation time on the conversion of benzyl alcohol and the selectivity for benzaldehyde.(b) The yield of methanol production (unit: mol g -1 ) as a function of irradiation time.(Reaction conditions: 30 ml of CH 3 CN, 1 mmol of benzyl alcohol, 50 mg of catalyst). Fig.4Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methanol coupled with alcohol oxidation with the 3 wt AgTiO 2 catalyst after three run cycles. Production yields of the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with selective benzyl alcohol oxidation under UV light irradiation using TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 catalysts a 2 to methanol coupled with alcohol oxidation is as follows: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methanol coupled with alcohol oxidation with the 3 wt AgTiO 2 catalyst a TiO 2 , stirred in 40 ml of CH 3 CN, under 0.1 MPa CO 2 and ultraviolet light (main wavelength of 365 nm) irradiation. a Reaction conditions: alcohol (1 mmol), 3 wt Agb RT: reaction time.cUnit:mol g-1.d Conversion, of alcohol.e Selectivity of benzaldehyde.f The ratio of the formation of methanol and aldehyde. This journal is The Royal Society of Chemistry 2015 a Reaction conditions: 40 ml of CH 3 CN, 1 mmol of benzyl alcohol.b Reaction time.c Reaction conditions: 40 ml of benzyl alcohol.d Reaction conditions: 40 ml of CH 3 CN, without benzyl alcohol. The support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21363014, 21106059 and 21103082) is gratefully acknowledged.This work was also supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (grant no.20142BCB23003 and 20143ACB21021).
